name: Turkish
register: Reverent contemporary Turkish matching the Kutsal Kitap: Yeni Çeviri (2001/2009, Kitab-ı Mukaddes Şirketi & Yeni Yaşam Yayınları) — the most-trusted modern Protestant Turkish Bible, which uses Tanrı for the generic deity and small-cap RAB for the covenant name. Warm, dignified, everyday-accessible Turkish rather than the archaic 1941 Kitabı Mukaddes register.
quote_convention: Turkish guillemets «…» for direct speech (as set in the Kutsal Kitap), with nested speech in ‹…›; contemporary editions also accept straight double quotes "…".
poetry_convention: Poetic books (Psalms, Proverbs, Prophets) set as indented verse, each stich on its own line with a hanging indent on runover, preserving Hebrew parallelism line-for-line, as in the Kutsal Kitap poetic sections.

glossary.gloss: our Father who is in the heavens
glossary.note: Uses the beloved Lord's Prayer wording (Matta 6:9, 'Göklerdeki Babamız') every Turkish believer knows — maximally filial and familiar; never 'Tanrımız'.
Baba + -mız (1pl possessive). The primary filial divine name; the address of a child, NEVER reverted to Tanrı / RAB.
Bare 'Baba' is the standard Turkish theological name for the first Person (Baba, Oğul, Kutsal Ruh); definiteness carried by context, not an article.
'Göksel' (heavenly) is the natural adjective form when no possessive is present; reserves 'Göklerdeki Babamız' for the possessive 'our'.
Retains the Aramaic cry with its Turkish gloss exactly as the Yeni Çeviri renders Markos 14:36 / Romalılar 8:15 / Galatyalılar 4:6 — the intimacy of the term is already canonical here.
REVERSION TOKEN — the Yeni Çeviri word for God-as-God. Use only where English keeps 'God'; never as a stand-in for Babamız.
REVERSION TOKEN — small-cap RAB is the Yeni Çeviri rendering of the covenant name. Distinct from generic 'Lord' for Jesus = Rab / Rab'bimiz. Never a substitute for Babamız.
Standard, universally recognized Turkish Bible term.
Relational genitive (Babamız + -ın … Ruh + -u), 'the Spirit belonging to our Father' — not the by-identity 'Kutsal Ruh'.
Fixed compound (insan + oğlu) used throughout the Turkish Gospels for Jesus' self-title.
Bare 'Oğul' pairs with 'Baba' in the Turkish Trinitarian formula; the eternal Son, Jesus.
Standard Christological title in the Yeni Çeviri. Kept as-is (not filialized to 'Baba'nın Oğlu') — native reviewer to confirm this contrast term stays with Tanrı.

glossary.gloss: the one and only / unique Son
glossary.note: 'biricik' = sole/one-and-only, exactly the Yeni Çeviri wording of Yuhanna 3:16 ('biricik Oğlunu verdi').

glossary.gloss: the reign / sovereignty of our Father
glossary.note: Filial form of the Yeni Çeviri's 'Tanrı'nın Egemenliği'; 'Egemenlik' (reign) is the modern Turkish choice over archaic 'Krallık' (kingdom).
The universal Turkish name for Jesus, often İsa Mesih in full.
Standard title for Jesus (İsa Mesih); Turkish uses the Semitic 'Mesih' rather than a Greek-derived form.
Same word as 'Christ' in Turkish; distinguished only by context (the awaited Deliverer vs. the title of Jesus).
The Yeni Çeviri term (Eski/Yeni Antlaşma = Old/New Testament); replaces archaic 'ahit'.
Renders hesed — 'vefa' carries the covenant-loyalty sense Turkish readers feel, pairing it with 'sevgi' (love). Reviewer to confirm vs. plain 'sadık sevgi'.
'şefkat' is gut-level parental tenderness — the precise sense of racham / splanchnizomai — distinct from 'merhamet' (mercy).
The Yeni Çeviri term for righteousness; 'adalet' (justice) is reserved for judicial contexts.
Exact Yeni Çeviri wording of Yuhanna 3:16 ('sonsuz yaşama kavuşsun').
The Yeni Çeviri phrasing of Yuhanna 3:3,7 ('Yeniden doğmadıkça'); inflected as 'yeniden doğmuş' where the participle is needed.
